questions.question: Why are hash indexes generally inefficient for range queries (e.g., WHERE column BETWEEN 10 AND 20)?
expectedPoints: Hash functions map keys to random buckets
Data is not stored in sorted order
Executing the query would require a full table scan of all buckets to find values within the range
This eliminates the performance benefit of the index

questions.question: Describe the process of inserting a new record into a table that uses an extendible hash index. What happens when a bucket overflows?
expectedPoints: The key of the new record is hashed to a bit string
The first 'd' (global depth) bits are used to find an entry in the directory
The directory entry points to a bucket where the (key, pointer) entry is stored
If the bucket overflows, it is split into two new buckets
The local depth of the original bucket is incremented
The records in the original bucket are rehashed using one more bit to redistribute them between the two new buckets
If the new local depth exceeds the global depth, the directory is doubled in size and the global depth is incremented by 1
The directory pointers are updated to point to the correct (new or old) buckets
